subject: Differentiation: Definition and Basic Derivative Rules

skills.skill: 2.1 Defining Average and Instantaneous Rates of Change at a Point
skills.prompt: This topic covers defining average and instantaneous rates of change for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-2.A, CHA-2.B, Unit 2, Topic 2.1). The average rate of change of f on [a,b] is [f(b)-f(a)]/(b-a), which is the slope of the secant line through (a,f(a)) and (b,f(b)). The instantaneous rate of change at x = a is the limit of the average rate of change as the interval shrinks: lim[h->0] [f(a+h)-f(a)]/h. Key concepts: secant lines approximate tangent lines as endpoints get closer; the instantaneous rate of change is the slope of the tangent line at a point; interpreting rates of change in real-world contexts (velocity, growth rate, marginal cost). Students should compute average rates from functions, tables, and graphs, and understand that the instantaneous rate requires a limiting process. AP exam note: students must be able to interpret the meaning of average and instantaneous rates in context, including units. Vocabulary: average rate of change, instantaneous rate of change, secant line, tangent line, difference quotient, slope.

skills.skill: 2.2 Defining the Derivative of a Function and Using Derivative Notation
skills.prompt: This topic covers the definition of the derivative using limits for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-2.B, CHA-2.C, Unit 2, Topic 2.2). The derivative of f at x = a is defined as f'(a) = lim[h->0] [f(a+h)-f(a)]/h, equivalently lim[x->a] [f(x)-f(a)]/(x-a). When this limit exists, it gives the instantaneous rate of change and the slope of the tangent line at x = a. The derivative function f'(x) is defined by applying this limit for general x. Key notation: f'(x), dy/dx, d/dx[f(x)], and y'. Students should compute derivatives from the limit definition for polynomial and simple functions. The derivative at a point can also be interpreted as the best linear approximation of f near that point. AP exam note: free-response questions may require students to set up and evaluate the limit definition, or explain what a derivative represents in context. Vocabulary: derivative, limit definition, difference quotient, instantaneous rate of change, tangent line, Leibniz notation, prime notation.

skills.skill: 2.3 Estimating Derivatives of a Function at a Point
skills.prompt: This topic covers estimating derivatives at a point for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-2.D, Unit 2, Topic 2.3). Students estimate f'(a) using slopes of secant lines from tables or graphs. From a table, use the difference quotient with values near a: f'(a) is approximately [f(a+h)-f(a)]/h for small h, or use the symmetric difference quotient [f(a+h)-f(a-h)]/(2h) for a better estimate. From a graph, estimate the slope of the tangent line by drawing it and computing rise/run. Key concepts: the estimate improves as the interval shrinks; the symmetric difference quotient often gives a more accurate estimate; students should choose appropriate nearby points from the table. This skill connects numerical and graphical understanding to the analytical definition of the derivative. AP exam note: free-response questions frequently provide tables of values and ask students to approximate a derivative, often requiring units and contextual interpretation. Vocabulary: estimate, secant line, difference quotient, symmetric difference quotient, slope, approximation.

skills.skill: 2.4 Connecting Differentiability and Continuity: Determining When Derivatives Do and Do Not Exist
skills.prompt: This topic covers the relationship between differentiability and continuity for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-2.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.4). Key theorem: if f is differentiable at x = c, then f is continuous at c. The converse is false: continuity does not guarantee differentiability. A function is not differentiable at points where: (1) there is a corner or cusp (left and right derivatives differ), (2) there is a vertical tangent (slope is infinite), (3) there is a discontinuity. Students should identify non-differentiable points from graphs by looking for sharp turns, vertical tangent lines, and jumps. Differentiability on an interval means the derivative exists at every point in the interval. Piecewise functions require checking that left-hand and right-hand derivatives match at boundary points. AP exam note: students may be asked to determine where a function is differentiable and justify using the relationship between differentiability and continuity. Vocabulary: differentiable, continuous, corner, cusp, vertical tangent, piecewise, left-hand derivative, right-hand derivative.

This topic covers the Power Rule for differentiation in AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-3.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.5). The Power Rule states: if f(x) = x^n, then f'(x) = n*x^(n-1) for any real number n. This extends to functions like f(x) = x^(1/2) (square root), f(x) = x^(-1) (reciprocal), and other rational and negative exponents. Students should rewrite radicals and reciprocals as power functions before applying the rule. Key applications: d/dx[x^3] = 3x^2, d/dx[sqrt(x)] = d/dx[x^(1/2)] = (1/2)x^(-1/2), d/dx[1/x^2] = d/dx[x^(-2)] = -2x^(-3). Combined with constant multiple and sum/difference rules (Topic 2.6), the Power Rule enables differentiation of all polynomial functions. Students should practice both finding derivatives and rewriting functions into power-rule-ready form. AP exam note: the Power Rule is foundational and is used extensively throughout the course. Errors in applying it (especially with negative and fractional exponents) are common. Vocabulary: Power Rule, exponent, polynomial, rational exponent, negative exponent, rewrite.

skills.skill: 2.6 Derivative Rules: Constant, Sum, Difference, and Constant Multiple
skills.prompt: This topic covers basic derivative rules for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-3.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.6). Rules: (1) Constant Rule: d/dx[c] = 0 for any constant c. (2) Constant Multiple Rule: d/dx[c*f(x)] = c*f'(x). (3) Sum Rule: d/dx[f(x)+g(x)] = f'(x)+g'(x). (4) Difference Rule: d/dx[f(x)-g(x)] = f'(x)-g'(x). These rules, combined with the Power Rule, allow differentiation of any polynomial: d/dx[3x^4 - 5x^2 + 7x - 2] = 12x^3 - 10x + 7. Students should understand that differentiation is a linear operation (it preserves addition and scalar multiplication). Key applications include finding equations of tangent lines, determining rates of change, and setting derivatives equal to zero to find critical points. AP exam note: these fundamental rules are used in virtually every derivative computation on the exam. Students must apply them fluently and accurately. Vocabulary: constant rule, constant multiple rule, sum rule, difference rule, linear operator, polynomial derivative.

skills.skill: 2.7 Derivatives of cos x, sin x, eˣ, and ln x
skills.prompt: This topic covers derivatives of basic transcendental functions for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-3.A, LIM-3.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.7). Key formulas: d/dx[sin x] = cos x, d/dx[cos x] = -sin x, d/dx[e^x] = e^x, d/dx[ln x] = 1/x. Students should memorize these derivatives and understand their derivations: the sine derivative uses lim[h->0] sin(h)/h = 1 and lim[h->0] (cos(h)-1)/h = 0; the exponential derivative follows from the definition of e as the base for which d/dx[e^x] = e^x; the natural log derivative follows from implicit differentiation of y = ln x (e^y = x). These transcendental derivatives appear frequently in combination with the product, quotient, and chain rules in later topics. AP exam note: these four derivatives are among the most commonly tested on the AP exam. Students should be able to combine them with other rules fluently. Vocabulary: transcendental function, sine, cosine, exponential, natural logarithm, derivative formula.

This topic covers the Product Rule for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-3.B, Unit 2, Topic 2.8). If f and g are differentiable, then d/dx[f(x)*g(x)] = f'(x)*g(x) + f(x)*g'(x). In Leibniz notation: d/dx[uv] = u'v + uv'. Key concepts: the derivative of a product is NOT the product of the derivatives; students must apply the rule correctly by differentiating each factor separately and summing. Students should practice with products involving polynomials, trigonometric, exponential, and logarithmic functions. Example: d/dx[x^2 sin x] = 2x sin x + x^2 cos x. The Product Rule can be extended to three or more factors. It is also useful in deriving other rules (e.g., the Quotient Rule can be derived from the Product Rule). AP exam note: the Product Rule is frequently tested both in isolation and in combination with the Chain Rule. Students should simplify their answers when possible. Vocabulary: Product Rule, factors, differentiate, derivative of a product, Leibniz notation.

This topic covers the Quotient Rule for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-3.B, Unit 2, Topic 2.9). If f and g are differentiable and g(x) != 0, then d/dx[f(x)/g(x)] = [f'(x)*g(x) - f(x)*g'(x)] / [g(x)]^2. Memory aid: 'low d-high minus high d-low over low squared.' Key concepts: the derivative of a quotient is NOT the quotient of the derivatives; the order of subtraction matters (it is not commutative); the denominator is always the square of the original denominator. Students should practice with rational functions and quotients involving transcendental functions. Example: d/dx[sin x / x] = [cos x * x - sin x * 1]/x^2. Sometimes rewriting a quotient using negative exponents and the Product Rule is an alternative approach. AP exam note: the Quotient Rule is commonly tested; sign errors and order errors are frequent mistakes. Students should verify their answers by checking special cases. Vocabulary: Quotient Rule, numerator, denominator, rational function, lo-d-hi, simplify.

skills.skill: 2.10 Finding the Derivatives of Tangent, Cotangent, Secant, and/or Cosecant Functions
skills.prompt: This topic covers derivatives of the remaining trigonometric functions for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-3.B, Unit 2, Topic 2.10). Key formulas: d/dx[tan x] = sec^2 x, d/dx[cot x] = -csc^2 x, d/dx[sec x] = sec x tan x, d/dx[csc x] = -csc x cot x. These are derived using the Quotient Rule applied to sin x/cos x (for tangent), cos x/sin x (for cotangent), 1/cos x (for secant), and 1/sin x (for cosecant). Students should memorize these formulas and notice the pattern: co-function derivatives have negative signs. These derivatives frequently appear in combination with the Chain Rule (e.g., d/dx[tan(3x)] = 3 sec^2(3x)). Understanding the domains of these functions (avoiding zeros of sin x and cos x) is important. AP exam note: while these derivatives appear less frequently than sin and cos, they are commonly tested in multiple-choice and are essential for integration in later units. Vocabulary: tangent, cotangent, secant, cosecant, Quotient Rule derivation, co-function, domain restriction.
